rationale: This is a canonical example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich phenol (resorcinol) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (zinc chloride) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4-dihydroxyacetophenone) after hydrolysis of the intermediate ketimine salt. The substrates are simple, acyclic where possible, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of this named reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a highly activated phenol, which is characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, distinguishing it from the more general Friedel-Crafts acylation that typically uses less activated arenes. The use of a nitrile as the acylating agent is the key defining feature, avoiding confusion with reactions using acyl halides or anhydrides. The simple, non-fused aromatic ring and acyclic nitrile prevent overlap with more complex annulation or heterocyclic synthesis reactions.
